text.title: Canonical Headless Invocation Shape
text.summary: The `claude -p` invocation pattern that orchestrators should standardise on for non-interactive runs.

section.title: Required flags for headless
section.p: A subprocess that disables the TUI, allowlists tools, caps turns, and emits structured events is the only safe shape for production.

rule.statement: Headless invocations MUST set `-p`/`--print`, an explicit `--allowedTools` list, `--max-turns`, and `--output-format stream-json --verbose` so the orchestrator can parse events line-by-line.
rule.rationale: Without `-p` the binary opens a TUI; without `--allowedTools` the agent stalls on permission prompts; without `--max-turns` a runaway loop can drain budget; without stream-json the orchestrator only sees the final blob.

section.reference: Reference wrapper that wires every required flag and budget logic.

example.code: claude -p "Fix failing test in tests/test_auth.py" \ --output-format stream-json --verbose \ --allowedTools "Read,Edit,Bash(pytest:*)" \ --max-turns 20 \ < /dev/null
example.note: Explicit allowlist, capped turns, closed stdin, stream-json output for orchestrator events.
No allowlist, no turn cap, no stream-json — the orchestrator cannot react to mid-run events and tool access is unbounded.

antipattern.description: Treating `claude -p` like a one-shot text generator and parsing only the final stdout blob.
antipattern.reason: Loses the cost/tool/event stream needed for budget enforcement, mid-run hand-off, and safety vetoes — see the stream-json-orchestration methodology.

see-also.link: How to drive an orchestrator state-machine off stream-json events.
